en.extract:
MacGregor's bowerbird (Amblyornis macgregoriae) is a medium-sized, up to 26 cm long, olive brown bowerbird of New Guinea's mountain forests, roughly the size and shape of an American robin or a Eurasian blackbird. The male is adorned with an erectile orange yellow crest, that is partly hidden until shown in courtship display. The unadorned female is similar to the male, but without the crest. Superb mimics, they are known for imitating other birds, pigs, rushing water, and even human speech.


Taxonomy
MacGregor's bowerbird was formally described in 1890 as Amblyornis macgregoriae by the English zoologist Charles Walter De Vis based on specimens collected in the Musgrave Range of southeast Papua New Guinea. The specific epithet was chosen to honour 'Lady Macgregor', wife of Sir William McGregor, Administrator of British New Guinea during 1888–98. Sir William's surname was originally, and thus formally, McGregor but he adopted the spelling MacGregor while in New Guinea as his personal preference.
Three subspecies are recognised:

A. m. mayri Hartert, EJO, 1930 – montane west-central and central New Guinea (Weyland, Nassau, and Jayawijaya mountains)
A. m. macgregoriae De Vis, CW, 1890 – montane east-central to northeastern (Adelbert Mountains) and southeastern New Guinea
A. m. germanus Rothschild, LW, 1910 – mountains of Huon Peninsula (northeastern New Guinea)
The subspecies A. m. germanus has sometimes been considered as a separate species, the Huon bowerbird.


Behaviour
The polygamous male builds a tower-like "maypole-type" bower, an elaborate courtship structure, with a central pole of twigs surrounded by a dish of moss with raised walls approximately 1 meter in diameter. He decorates the twigs of the maypole with flowers, fruits, insects and other objects. The diet consists mainly of fruits and insects.

When a female comes in proximity to the bower, the male struts and calls, and opens his crest to display its full color. Hiding the crest except during sexual display is thought to minimize his vulnerability to predators.


Conservation status
Widespread and common throughout its range, MacGregor's bowerbird is evaluated as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species.


References


External links
BirdLife Species Factsheet

[[File:Huon Bowerbird (Amblyornis macgregoriae ssp. germana) (cropped).jpg|thumb|Subspecies of MacGregor's bowerbird, the Huon Bowerbird (''Amblyornis macgregoriae germana'')]]
'''MacGregor's bowerbird''' ('''''Amblyornis macgregoriae''''') is a medium-sized, up to 26 cm long, olive brown [[bowerbird]] of [[New Guinea]]'s mountain forests, roughly the size and shape of an [[American robin]] or a [[Eurasian blackbird]]. The male is adorned with an erectile orange yellow crest, that is partly hidden until shown in courtship display. The unadorned female is similar to the male, but without the crest. Superb mimics, they are known for imitating other birds, pigs, rushing water, and even human speech.

==Taxonomy==
MacGregor's bowerbird was [[species description|formally described]] in 1890 as ''Amblyornis macgregoriae'' by the English zoologist [[Charles Walter De Vis]] based on specimens collected in the Musgrave Range of southeast Papua New Guinea.<ref>{{ cite book | last=De Vis | first=Charles Walter | author-link=Charles Walter De Vis | date=1890 | chapter=Appendix G. Report on birds from British New Guinea | editor-last=MacGregor | editor-first=William MacGregor | editor-link=William MacGregor| title=British New Guinea. Annual report by Her Majesty's administrator of the government, from 4th September 1888 to 30th June 1889 | location=Melbourne | publisher=Robert S Brain Government Printer | pages=58-61 [61] | url=https://nla.gov.au/nla.obj-82611601/view?partId=nla.obj-82611674#}}<!-- page 59 is missing--></ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Greenway | editor2-first=James C. Jr | year=1962 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=15 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | location=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=176 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14485549 }}</ref> The specific epithet was chosen to honour 'Lady Macgregor', wife of Sir [[William MacGregor|William McGregor]], Administrator of British New Guinea during 1888–98. Sir William's surname was originally, and thus formally, McGregor but he adopted the spelling MacGregor while in New Guinea as his personal preference.<ref name="Frith 2020">{{cite journal |last1=Frith |first1=Clifford B. |title=The New Guinea bird names Macgregor's Bowerbird and Macgregor's Honeyeater |journal=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club |date=22 June 2020 |volume=140 |issue=2 |pages=214–216 |doi=10.25226/bboc.v140i2.2020.a12 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=macgregoriae | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=macgregoriae | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=21 January 2026 }}</ref>

Three [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=avilist>{{ cite web | author=AviList Core Team | date=2025 | title=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025 | doi=10.2173/avilist.v2025 | doi-access=free | url=http://www.avilist.org/checklist/v2025/ | access-date=21 January 2026 }}</ref>
* ''A. m. mayri'' [[Ernst Hartert|Hartert, EJO]], 1930 – montane west-central and central [[New Guinea]] (Weyland, Nassau, and Jayawijaya mountains)
* ''A. m. macgregoriae'' [[Charles Walter De Vis|De Vis, CW]], 1890 – montane east-central to northeastern ([[Adelbert Range|Adelbert Mountains]]) and southeastern [[New Guinea]]
* ''A. m. germanus'' [[Walter Rothschild, 2nd Baron Rothschild|Rothschild, LW]], 1910 – mountains of [[Huon Peninsula]] (northeastern [[New Guinea]])

The subspecies ''A. m. germanus'' has sometimes been considered as a separate species, the Huon bowerbird.<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=February 2025 | title=Lyrebirds, scrubbirds, bowerbirds, Australasian treecreepers, Australasian wrens | work=IOC World Bird List Version 15.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/lyrebirds/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=21 January 2026 }}</ref>

==Behaviour==
The [[Animal sexual behaviour#Polygamy|polygamous]] male builds a tower-like "maypole-type" bower, an elaborate courtship structure, with a central pole of twigs surrounded by a dish of moss with raised walls approximately 1 meter in diameter. He decorates the twigs of the maypole with flowers, fruits, insects and other objects. The diet consists mainly of fruits and insects.
[[File:MacGregor's Bowerbird (Amblyornis macgregoriae) bower.jpg|thumb|Remains of a MacGregor's Bowerbird bower]]
When a female comes in proximity to the bower, the male struts and calls, and opens his crest to display its full color. Hiding the crest except during sexual display is thought to minimize his vulnerability to predators.

==Conservation status==
Widespread and common throughout its range, MacGregor's bowerbird is evaluated as Least Concern on the [[IUCN Red List]] of Threatened Species.<ref name=iucn/>

zh.extract:
冠园丁鸟（学名：Amblyornis macgregoriae），是雀形目园丁鸟科的一种鸟类。
冠园丁鸟体重约117.47克，翼长约131.6毫米，嘴峰长约26.7毫米，喙宽度约7.6毫米，喙厚度约8.8毫米，跗蹠长约37.3毫米，尾长约88.4毫米。冠园丁鸟在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在密集的高大树木组成的森林中，食性为草食性，主要食物来源是水果。


亚种
A. m. mayri：分布于新几内亚的Weyland山脉、拿骚湾和Jayawijaya山脉。
A. m. macgregoriae：分布于新几内亚中央山脉、休恩半岛和阿德尔伯特山脉。
A. m. germana：分布于新几内亚东北部休恩半岛的山区，分类地位有争议，也被认为是独立物种休恩亭鸟。
A. m. kombok：分布于巴布亚新几内亚芒特哈根。
A. m. lecroyae：分布于巴布亚新几内亚东北部博薩維山。
A. m. nubicola：分布于巴布亚新几内亚东南部欧文斯坦利山脉。
